---
title: "Data Shape Conventions"
author: "Dereck Mezquita"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Data Shape Conventions}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>",
  eval = FALSE
)
```

Alpaca's JSON responses sometimes carry nested arrays and objects. The
`alpaca` package never returns a `data.table` with a list column — every
method follows one rule:

> **Identify the entity for the endpoint, and return one row per entity.**

A trade has a row, an article has a row, an asset has a row. Anything
nested *under* the entity becomes either a flat column on the same row
or an additional row on a different axis — never an opaque list cell.
The exact treatment depends on the nested shape.

## The four treatments

There are four shapes a nested field can take. Each gets a different
treatment.

### Treatment A — `;` collapse for arrays of strings

When the entity has a variable-length array of plain strings (tags,
codes, permissions, identifiers), we join them into one character
column with a `;` separator.

```r
asset <- market$get_asset("AAPL")
asset$attributes
#> "fractional_eh_enabled;has_options;overnight_tradable"
```

Filter with `grepl`:

```r
options_assets <- assets[grepl("has_options", attributes)]
```

Recover the original character vector by splitting:

```r
strsplit(asset$attributes, ";", fixed = TRUE)[[1]]
#> [1] "fractional_eh_enabled" "has_options" "overnight_tradable"
```

The separator is `;` rather than `,` because semicolons are far less
likely to appear *inside* one of the values themselves. For fields where
a value could legally contain `;` — currently just news image URLs,
where `;` is a valid URL sub-delimiter — the value is percent-encoded to
`%3B` before joining and you recover via `URLdecode()` per element:

```r
news <- market$get_news(symbols = "AAPL", limit = 1)
urls  <- strsplit(news$image_urls[1], ";", fixed = TRUE)[[1]]
urls  <- vapply(urls, URLdecode, character(1))
```

Where it's used:

- `get_assets()` / `get_asset()` — `attributes`
- `get_latest_trade()` / `get_trades()` / `get_latest_trades_multi()` —
  `conditions`
- `get_latest_quote()` / `get_quotes()` / `get_latest_quotes_multi()` —
  `conditions`
- `get_news()` — `symbols`, `image_sizes`, `image_urls`

### Treatment B — Long format for arrays of objects

When the array elements are themselves records (each has a `price`, a
`size`, a `symbol`, etc.), we explode to one row per element with parent
fields replicated. A position-index column is added where order matters
(`level`, `leg_index`).

```r
ob <- market$get_crypto_orderbook("BTC/USD")
ob
#>     symbol side level    price  size           timestamp
#>     <char> <char> <int>   <num> <num>              <POSc>
#>  1: BTC/USD  bid     1 42950.50  0.5 2024-01-15 20:00:00
#>  2: BTC/USD  bid     2 42949.00  1.2 2024-01-15 20:00:00
#>  3: BTC/USD  ask     1 42951.00  0.3 2024-01-15 20:00:00
#>  4: BTC/USD  ask     2 42952.50  0.8 2024-01-15 20:00:00
```

Where it's used:

- `get_crypto_orderbook()` — `bids` / `asks` levels.
- `get_watchlists()` / `get_watchlist()` / `add_watchlist()` /
  `modify_watchlist()` / `add_watchlist_symbol()` /
  `cancel_watchlist_symbol()` / `cancel_watchlist()` — `assets` with
  `asset_` prefix on each row.

### Treatment B′ — Parent + leg rows for multi-leg orders

A variant of Treatment B that applies to multi-leg orders (`bracket`,
`oco`, `oto`, `mleg`). The parent and each leg get their own row in a
single flat table; two helper columns disambiguate:

- `leg_index` (integer): `NA` on the parent row, `1, 2, …` on each leg.
- `parent_order_id` (character): `NA` on the parent row, the parent's
  `id` on each leg row.

```r
# A bracket order: 1 parent + 2 legs = 3 rows
o <- trading$add_order(
  symbol = "AAPL", side = "buy", type = "limit",
  time_in_force = "gtc", qty = 1, limit_price = 1,
  order_class = "bracket",
  take_profit = list(limit_price = 500),
  stop_loss   = list(stop_price = 0.5, limit_price = 0.4)
)
nrow(o)                                  # 3
o[is.na(parent_order_id), .(id, side)]   # the parent
o[parent_order_id == o[is.na(parent_order_id), id],
  .(leg_index, side, limit_price, stop_price)]  # the two legs
```

A simple order returns a single row with both helper columns `NA`.

Where it's used:

- `add_order()` / `get_order()` / `get_orders()` /
  `get_order_by_client_id()` / `modify_order()`.

### Treatment C — Wide prefix for fixed-schema nested objects

When the nested data is an *object* (not an array) with a known fixed
key set, we flatten the inner fields into `parent_child` columns on the
same row. The parent stays one row.

```r
acct <- account$get_account()
acct$admin_configurations_max_options_trading_level   # was nested under admin_configurations
#> 2
acct$user_configurations_dtbp_check                   # was nested under user_configurations
#> "both"
```

Where it's used:

- `get_account()` — `admin_configurations_*`, `user_configurations_*`.
- `get_snapshot()` / `get_snapshots_multi()` — `latest_trade_*`,
  `latest_quote_*`, `minute_bar_*`, `daily_bar_*`, `prev_daily_bar_*`.

## One hybrid

`get_snapshot()` is Treatment C with one inner Treatment A. The
top-level object is wide-flattened to `latest_trade_*` /
`latest_quote_*` / `*_bar_*` columns; the inner `c` arrays on the trade
and quote sections become semicolon-joined `latest_trade_conditions` /
`latest_quote_conditions` columns. The `c` field on the bar sections is
the **close price** (a scalar number), so it's not touched — it ends up
as a numeric `*_close` column.

```r
snap <- market$get_snapshot("AAPL")
snap$latest_trade_conditions   # "@;T"          — Treatment A inside the C flatten
snap$latest_quote_conditions   # "R"            — same
snap$minute_bar_close          # 304.7          — left alone, numeric
```

## Two cross-cutting rules

These apply to every method, regardless of which treatment it uses.

**Empty arrays become `NA`, not list cells.** An asset with no
attributes, a trade with no condition codes, an article with no images
— all set the relevant column to `NA_character_`. Downstream code that
does `is.na()` keeps working; no list-column gymnastics.

**Empty responses return an empty `data.table`.** No synthetic stub
rows. `nrow()` is the honest count of "things I got back."

## What was *not* done

We considered but didn't end up needing **list of two data.tables** —
returning e.g. `list(orders = ..., legs = ...)` keyed by a foreign key
for genuinely heterogeneous nested arrays. Every nested field across
the package fits into one of the four treatments above. The "list of
data.tables" escape hatch is held in reserve if a future endpoint forces
it.

## Why this matters across exchanges

The same convention is being applied to the sibling `binance` and
`kucoin` packages. A trader who hops between them can rely on:

- the same canonical column names (`symbol`, `timestamp`, `price`,
  `size`, `side`, `conditions`, …),
- the same row-shape contract (entity-per-row, never a list column),
- the same recovery idioms (`grepl`, `strsplit`, `URLdecode`, the
  `is.na(parent_order_id)` filter for multi-leg orders).

That predictability is what makes the connector layer (`exchanges`
package, on top of `tradebot-core`) easy to write: each raw wrapper
returns data the connector can re-shape without having to special-case
list columns or wrap-and-unwrap nested structures.
